mechanism_of_action: Colistin sulfate causes ultrastructural alterations in E. coli in three stages: Stage 1 involves disaxialization and margination of nuclear material, and invasion of nuclear areas by cytoplasmic tufts, with no cellular content loss. Stage 2 involves loss of nuclear material, loss of cytoplasmic granularity, increased cytoplasmic electron density, and agglomeration of cytoplasm into packed tufts, with no cytoplasmic material loss. Stage 3 involves loss of cytoplasmic materials, but persistence of cell wall, plasma membrane, and mesosomes. These alterations are speculated to be caused by an altered intracellular ionic milieu due to a primary effect on the plasma membrane, affecting selective permeability.
